sections.title: Permissions & ownership
sections.subtitle: Control who can read, write, and run every file

lessons.title: How Linux permissions work
lessons.description: Understand the three permission types and who they apply to

steps.label: Read, write, execute
steps.text:
Every file and directory in Linux has three permission types:

<hl>r</hl> — read: view the file's contents
<hl>w</hl> — write: modify or delete the file
<hl>x</hl> — execute: run the file as a program

These apply to three groups of people:
<hl>u</hl> — user (the file's owner)
<hl>g</hl> — group (members of the file's group)
<hl>o</hl> — others (everyone else)

Run <cmd>ls -l</cmd> to see permissions on every file.

steps.label: Quick check
steps.question: In the string -rw-r--r--, what permissions does the file owner have?

feedback.ok: Correct! rw- means read and write. The dash means no execute permission.
feedback.bad: Look at positions 2-4: r=read, w=write, -=no execute. So the owner has read and write only.

steps.label: Fill in the blank
sentence: The permission string has 10 characters. The first character shows the
type, then three groups of three characters follow.

feedback.ok: Correct! The first character is the file type: - for regular file, d for directory, l for symlink.
feedback.bad: The first character is the file type: dash for regular file, d for directory, l for symlink.

steps.label: Reading permissions
steps.question: A file shows drwxr-x---. What does the leading 'd' mean?

feedback.ok: Correct! d means it's a directory. A regular file shows - instead.
feedback.bad: The first character is the file type. d means directory, - means regular file, l means symlink.

steps.label: Write it yourself
steps.prompt: List the files in the current directory with their permission details.
steps.hint: Use ls with the -l flag (long format) to see permissions.

output.ok:
<success>✓ Correct!</success>
<info>total 32
-rw-r--r-- 1 user staff  420 Mar 10 notes.txt
-rwxr-xr-x 1 user staff 1024 Mar 10 script.sh
drwxr-xr-x 2 user staff   64 Mar 10 projects/</info>
output.bad:
<e>✗ Not quite.</e>
<info>Hint: ls -l shows files in long format, revealing permissions, owner, size, and date.</info>

lessons.title: Changing permissions — chmod
lessons.description: Use symbolic notation to grant and revoke permissions

steps.label: chmod with symbols
steps.text:
<hl>chmod</hl> changes file permissions. The symbolic format is:

<cmd>chmod [who][+/-/=][permission] file</cmd>

<hl>who</hl>: u (user/owner), g (group), o (others), a (all)
<hl>+</hl> adds, <hl>-</hl> removes, <hl>=</hl> sets exactly

Examples:
<cmd>chmod u+x script.sh</cmd>   — give owner execute
<cmd>chmod go-w file.txt</cmd>   — remove write from group & others
<cmd>chmod a+r readme.txt</cmd>  — give everyone read

steps.label: Quick check
steps.question: What does chmod go-w data.csv do?
options: Give group and others write permission
Remove write permission from group and others
Remove write permission from the owner only
Give write permission to the owner

feedback.ok: Correct! go means group and others, - removes, w is write.
feedback.bad: Break it down: go = group and others, - = remove, w = write permission.

steps.label: Reorder the command
steps.instruction: Make a shell script executable by its owner only:

feedback.ok: Correct! chmod first, then the permission change, then the target file.
feedback.bad: Order: command (chmod) → permission change (u+x) → target file (deploy.sh).

steps.label: Fill in the blank
sentence: To remove execute permission from everyone, you would run: chmod

feedback.ok: Correct! a means all (user, group, and others). chmod a-x removes execute from everyone.
feedback.bad: a stands for 'all' — it targets user, group, and others simultaneously.

steps.label: Write it yourself
steps.prompt: Remove read and write permission from 'others' on the file secret.txt.
steps.hint: Use chmod, o for others, - to remove, then rw for read and write.

output.ok:
<success>✓ Correct! secret.txt is now private to owner and group.</success>
<info>-rw-r----- 1 user staff 256 Mar 10 secret.txt</info>
output.bad:
<e>✗ Not quite.</e>
<info>Try: chmod o-rw secret.txt
o = others, - = remove, rw = read+write</info>

lessons.title: Octal permissions
lessons.description: Use three-digit numbers to set permissions precisely

steps.label: Octal notation
steps.text:
Instead of symbols, you can use a 3-digit number with <cmd>chmod</cmd>.

Each digit represents user, group, others — and is the sum of:
<hl>4</hl> = read
<hl>2</hl> = write
<hl>1</hl> = execute

So <hl>7</hl> = 4+2+1 = rwx (full access)
<hl>5</hl> = 4+0+1 = r-x (read & execute)
<hl>4</hl> = 4+0+0 = r-- (read only)
<hl>6</hl> = 4+2+0 = rw- (read & write)

Common patterns:
<cmd>chmod 755 script.sh</cmd>  — owner: rwx, group: r-x, others: r-x
<cmd>chmod 644 readme.txt</cmd> — owner: rw-, group: r--, others: r--

steps.label: Calculate octal
steps.question: What octal value gives read+write permissions (and nothing else)?

feedback.ok: Correct! 4 (read) + 2 (write) + 0 (no execute) = 6.
feedback.bad: Add up: read=4, write=2, execute=1. Read+write only = 4+2 = 6.

steps.label: Decode octal
steps.question: What permissions does chmod 644 give the file owner?

feedback.ok: Correct! The first digit 6 = 4+2 = read + write for the owner.
feedback.bad: The first digit is for the owner. 6 = 4+2 = read + write. No execute.

steps.label: Fill in the blank
sentence: A file with permissions rwxr-xr-x in octal notation is

feedback.ok: Correct! rwx=7, r-x=5, r-x=5 → 755.
feedback.bad: Calculate each group: rwx = 4+2+1 = 7, r-x = 4+0+1 = 5, r-x = 5. Total: 755.

steps.label: Write it yourself
steps.prompt: Set app.py so the owner can read, write and execute; group can read and execute; others can only read.
steps.hint: Owner: rwx=7, Group: r-x=5, Others: r--=4. Put them together: 754.

output.bad:
<e>✗ Not quite.</e>
<info>Owner rwx=7, Group r-x=5, Others r--=4 → chmod 754 app.py</info>

lessons.title: Ownership — chown & chgrp
lessons.description: Change who owns a file and which group it belongs to

steps.label: chown and chgrp
steps.text:
Every file has an <hl>owner</hl> (a user) and a <hl>group</hl>.

<cmd>chown user file</cmd>        — change the owner
<cmd>chown user:group file</cmd>  — change owner and group at once
<cmd>chgrp group file</cmd>       — change the group only

You usually need <hl>sudo</hl> to change ownership on files you don't own.

<cmd>chown -R user:group dir/</cmd>  — the <hl>-R</hl> flag applies changes recursively to everything inside a directory.

steps.label: Quick check
steps.question: What does chown alice:devs project.sh do?
options: Changes the file permissions to allow alice and devs access
Sets alice as the owner and devs as the group
Adds alice to the devs group
Removes alice from the file's access list

feedback.ok: Correct! The format is chown owner:group — alice becomes the owner, devs becomes the group.
feedback.bad: chown owner:group sets the owner and the group. alice is the new owner, devs is the new group.

steps.label: Fill in the blank
sentence: To change ownership of a whole directory tree recursively, add the

feedback.ok: Correct! -R means recursive — it applies chown to the directory and everything inside it.
feedback.bad: -R (capital R) makes chown work recursively on all files and subdirectories.

steps.label: Reorder the command
steps.instruction: Change the group of logs/ and all its contents to the 'admin' group:

feedback.ok: Correct! chgrp then -R flag then the group name then the target.
feedback.bad: Order: command (chgrp) → flag (-R) → group name (admin) → target directory (logs/).

steps.label: Write it yourself
steps.prompt: Change the owner of server.conf to 'root' and the group to 'sysadmin'.
steps.hint: Use chown owner:group filename. You may need sudo for system files.

output.bad:
<e>✗ Not quite.</e>
<info>Try: sudo chown root:sysadmin server.conf</info>

lessons.title: Default permissions — umask
lessons.description: Control the default permissions assigned to new files and directories

steps.label: What is umask?
steps.text:
<hl>umask</hl> sets the default permissions that are <hl>removed</hl> when you create a new file or directory.

The starting point is:
  Files:       <hl>666</hl> (rw-rw-rw-)
  Directories: <hl>777</hl> (rwxrwxrwx)

The umask value is subtracted:
  umask <hl>022</hl> → files get 644 (666-022), dirs get 755 (777-022)
  umask <hl>077</hl> → files get 600, dirs get 700 (private)

Run <cmd>umask</cmd> to see the current value. Change it by running <cmd>umask 027</cmd>. Put it in <cmd>~/.bashrc</cmd> to make it permanent.

steps.label: Calculate umask result
steps.question: With umask 022, what permissions do new directories get?

feedback.ok: Correct! Directories start at 777, subtract 022 = 755 (rwxr-xr-x).
feedback.bad: Directories start at 777. 777 minus 022 = 755 (rwxr-xr-x).

steps.label: Private files
steps.question: Which umask value makes new files readable and writable only by their owner?

feedback.ok: Correct! umask 077 removes all group and other permissions. Files get 600 (rw-------).
feedback.bad: umask 077 removes rwx from group and others. Files start at 666, minus 077 = 600 (owner only).

steps.label: Fill in the blank
sentence: , rather than adding them like chmod does.

feedback.ok: Correct! umask works by masking (removing) bits from the default permissions.
feedback.bad: umask subtracts from the default permission set (666 for files, 777 for directories).

steps.label: Write it yourself
steps.hint: Just type the command name with no arguments.

lessons.title: Special permission bits
lessons.description: Learn setuid, setgid, and the sticky bit

steps.label: setuid, setgid, sticky bit
steps.text:
Beyond rwx, Linux has three special bits:

<hl>setuid (s on user execute)</hl>
When set on an executable, it runs as the file's <hl>owner</hl>, not the person who ran it. Used by commands like <cmd>passwd</cmd> that need root access.

<hl>setgid (s on group execute)</hl>
On a directory: new files inside inherit the directory's group instead of the creator's group. Great for shared project folders.

<hl>sticky bit (t on others execute)</hl>
On a directory: users can only delete their <hl>own</hl> files. Used on <cmd>/tmp</cmd> so users can't delete each other's temp files.

<warn>setuid on scripts is ignored by Linux for security reasons.</warn>

steps.label: Identify the sticky bit
steps.question: In the permission string drwxrwxrwt, what does the 't' at the end indicate?
options: The sticky bit is set — users can only delete their own files

feedback.ok: Correct! t (sticky bit) on a directory means only the file's owner can delete it.
feedback.bad: The t at the end of the permission string is the sticky bit. It prevents users from deleting other users' files.

steps.label: Identify setuid
steps.question: The /usr/bin/passwd file shows -rwsr-xr-x. What does the 's' in the user execute position mean?
options: The file can be run by anyone
The setuid bit is set — it runs as root regardless of who runs it

feedback.ok: Correct! s in the user execute position = setuid. passwd runs as root so it can update /etc/shadow.
feedback.bad: s in the user execute position is the setuid bit. The program runs with the owner's privileges (root here).

steps.label: Fill in the blank
sentence: To set the sticky bit on a shared directory, run: chmod

feedback.ok: Correct! chmod +t or chmod 1777 both set the sticky bit.
feedback.bad: Use chmod +t dirname or chmod 1777 dirname to set the sticky bit.

steps.label: setgid on directories
steps.question: You set setgid on a shared project directory. A new file is created inside. What group does it get?
options: The group of the person who created the file
The group of the directory itself

feedback.ok: Correct! With setgid, new files inherit the directory's group — useful for team collaboration.
feedback.bad: setgid on a directory makes all new files inside inherit the directory's group, not the creator's.

lessons.title: Section 4 review
lessons.description: Test your understanding of permissions and ownership

steps.label: Review
steps.question: What does -rwxr-x--- tell you about group permissions?

feedback.ok: Correct! Positions 5-7 are group: r-x = read and execute, no write.
feedback.bad: Positions 5-7 (r-x) represent group permissions: read=yes, write=no, execute=yes.

steps.label: Review
steps.question: Which octal value gives the owner all permissions, and no permissions to anyone else?

feedback.ok: Correct! 7 = rwx for owner, 0 = no permissions for group, 0 = no permissions for others.
feedback.bad: 700: owner gets 7 (rwx), group gets 0 (---), others get 0 (---). Only the owner can access it.

steps.label: Review
sentence: To give the file's group write permission, run: chmod

feedback.ok: Correct! g = group, + = add, w = write.
feedback.bad: g+w targets the group specifically. u=user, g=group, o=others, a=all.

steps.label: Review
steps.question: You want to transfer ownership of a file to user 'deploy'. Which command do you use?

feedback.ok: Correct! chown changes the file owner.
feedback.bad: chown is the command to change file ownership. chmod changes permissions, chgrp changes group.

steps.label: Review
steps.instruction: Make script.sh executable by everyone using octal notation:

feedback.ok: Correct! chmod then the octal value then the target file.
feedback.bad: Order: command (chmod) → octal value (755) → target file (script.sh).

steps.label: Review
steps.question: With umask 027, what permissions do new files get?

feedback.ok: Correct! Files start at 666, minus 027 = 640 (rw-r-----).
feedback.bad: Files default to 666. Subtract umask 027: 666 - 027 = 640 (rw-r-----).
